title: The Branch of Jesse
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 12–15-24 -AM- The Branch of Jesse - Isaiah 11_1—11 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Advent and the Context of Isaiah 11
subpoints: Advent series: Old Testament prophecy (this week), New Testament historical passage (next week), Christ's return (following week)
Isaiah 11 is part of the "Gospel according to Isaiah" (chapters 7-12)
Three messianic prophecies amid judgment: virgin birth (7:14), the Son given (9:5-6), the Branch of Jesse (11:1)
Historical context: northern kingdom taken by Assyria (722 BC); southern kingdom threatened and later taken by Babylon (607 BC)
Isaiah 11 is a ray of sunshine in proclamations of doom: a fresh green shoot rising from a fallen tree

outline.title: The Person of the Branch: His Identity
subpoints: Verse 1: A shoot from the stem of Jesse, a branch from his roots — a new Davidic king
Verse 10: The same shoot is also the root of the root — both man and God (2 Sam. 7)
Paul confirms in Romans 15 that Isaiah 11:10 refers to the ministry of Christ to the Gentiles
Jesus is both the shoot from Jesse and the creator of Jesse — a pointer to the two natures of Christ

outline.title: The Spirit Upon the Branch: His Empowerment
subpoints: Verse 2: Three pairs of characteristics — wisdom and understanding, counsel and might, knowledge and fear of the Lord
1 Corinthians 1:30-31: Jesus became for us wisdom from God, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption
Luke 4:14-21: Jesus returns to Galilee by the power of the Spirit, reads Isaiah 61 in Nazareth, and declares it fulfilled
His delight was in the fear of the Lord (verse 3) — joyful fear demonstrated in His life

outline.title: The Work of the Branch: Righteous Judgment
subpoints: Verses 3-5: He will not judge by appearances or hearsay but by the heart
He will judge the poor with righteousness and slay the wicked with the breath of His lips
He is doubly belted (v. 5) — ready to act in righteousness and faithfulness on our behalf
John 2:24-25: Jesus knows what is in men; He is the judge who knows our hearts
Romans: God in Jesus is both judge and justifier

outline.title: The Kingdom of the Branch: Renewed Creation
subpoints: Verses 6-9: wolf and lamb, leopard and goat, child and serpent — a picture of creation restored
Old Testament prophets often did not distinguish between the first and second coming of Christ
Romans 8:18-23: creation waits for the redemption of the body; the curse will be fully lifted at Christ's return
Verse 8: the child plays with the serpent — the seed of the woman (Gen. 3:15) has crushed the serpent's head

outline.title: The Banner and the Rest of the Branch
subpoints: Verse 10: the root of Jesse will stand as a banner to the nations (all peoples, not just Gentiles)
The banner that draws all people is the cross: "If I be lifted up, I will attract all people to myself"
The Branch provides a place of rest for the nations
Matthew 11:28-30: "Come to Me... and I will give you rest. For My yoke is easy and My burden is light"

summary: In this Advent sermon, Pastor Tito Lyro expounds Isaiah 11:1-10 as a messianic prophecy fulfilled in the Lord Jesus Christ, confirmed by Paul's quotation of verse 10 in Romans 15. Set against the backdrop of Isaiah's proclamations of judgment upon unfaithful Judah and Israel — and the looming threat of Assyrian conquest — the chapter presents a tiny green shoot rising from an apparently dead stump, representing the covenant promise of God that a son of David would reign forever. The sermon examines the identity of the Branch (both the shoot from Jesse and the root of Jesse, pointing to Christ's two natures), His empowerment by the Spirit in all wisdom, counsel, and knowledge (demonstrated in Luke 4 when He declares Isaiah 61 fulfilled), His work as a righteous judge who discerns the heart, and His ultimate establishment of a renewed creation from which the curse is lifted. The sermon closes on the rest the Branch provides: His banner is the cross, and His invitation is "Come to Me and I will give you rest."
transcript:
## Isaiah 11:1-10 — The Branch of Jesse

As you may have realized, we are in the middle of the Advent season. We will take the next three sermons to consider the Comings of Christ. This morning we consider an Old Testament prophecy concerning Christ. Next Lord's Day morning, a New Testament historical passage. Then the Lord's Day afternoon, the reminder that Jesus is coming back for His church.

We read Isaiah chapter 11, verses 1 through 10:

"There shall come forth a Rod from the stem of Jesse, and a Branch shall grow out of his roots. The Spirit of the Lord shall rest upon Him, the Spirit of wisdom and understanding, the Spirit of counsel and might, the Spirit of knowledge and of the fear of the Lord. His delight is in the fear of the Lord, and He shall not judge by the sight of His eyes, nor decide by the hearing of His ears; but with righteousness He shall judge the poor, and decide with equity for the meek of the earth. He shall strike the earth with the rod of His mouth, and with the breath of His lips He shall slay the wicked. Righteousness shall be the belt of His loins, and faithfulness the belt of His waist. The wolf also shall dwell with the lamb, the leopard shall lie down with the young goat, the calf and the young lion and the fatling together; and a little child shall lead them. The cow and the bear shall graze; their young ones shall lie down together; and the lion shall eat straw like the ox. The nursing child shall play by the cobra's hole, and the weaned child shall put his hand in the viper's den. They shall not hurt nor destroy in all My holy mountain, for the earth shall be full of the knowledge of the Lord as the waters cover the sea. And in that day there shall be a Root of Jesse, who shall stand as a banner to the people; for the Gentiles shall seek Him, and His resting place shall be glorious."

## The Context: Isaiah's Gospel Amid Judgment

This is a prophecy about Jesus Christ. Paul confirms this in Romans 15, where he quotes Isaiah 11:10 as a proof text for the ministry of Christ in bringing the Gentiles to salvation. We can be confident that what we read in Isaiah 11 is a description of the person and work of our Lord Jesus Christ.

No prophecy is given in a vacuum. In the history of Israel, after the reign of Solomon, the kingdom divided — ten tribes to the north (Israel/Ephraim) and two to the south (Judah), with Jerusalem and the temple. The northern kingdom was always rebellious, worshiping idols from the beginning. The southern kingdom had occasional revivals, but it too was mostly a rebellious people. Isaiah's prophecy addresses both kingdoms' coming judgment — Israel would be taken captive by Assyria in 722 BC, and Judah by Babylon beginning in 607 BC.

Isaiah 11 is a little ray of sunshine in the midst of proclamations of doom. The imagery is of a tree that has been cut down — all the trees of Lebanon are gone, utter devastation. And it is in the midst of this devastation that this little green shoot, this little green shoot of life, comes up. That is the Lord Jesus Christ.

## The Person: Shoot from Jesse and Root of Jesse

Verse 1: "There shall come forth a Rod from the stem of Jesse, and a Branch shall grow out of his roots." Jesse was David's father. This Branch is one like David — fulfilling the promise God made to David in 2 Samuel 7, that a son of David would reign forever.

But verse 10 adds a remarkable statement: "In that day there shall be a Root of Jesse." The same shoot is not only coming from the root — He is also the root of the root. The Messiah is both the Son of David and the Creator of David. He is from Jesse, and He is also the creator of Jesse. This is the two natures of Christ woven throughout Scripture: truly man and truly God.

## The Spirit: His Empowerment

Verse 2: "The Spirit of the Lord shall rest upon Him, the Spirit of wisdom and understanding, the Spirit of counsel and might, the Spirit of knowledge and of the fear of the Lord."

Three pairs of characteristics describe this anointed One: wisdom and understanding, counsel and might, knowledge and the fear of the Lord. Paul says in 1 Corinthians 1:30-31 that Jesus became for us wisdom from God, and righteousness, and sanctification, and redemption. In Acts 10, Peter preaches that Jesus was anointed with the Spirit and with power.

In Luke 4, after His wilderness temptation, Jesus goes to Nazareth, is given the scroll of Isaiah, and reads Isaiah 61: "The Spirit of the Lord is upon Me, because He has anointed Me to preach the gospel to the poor." He closes the scroll, sits down, and says: "Today this scripture is fulfilled in your hearing." The people understood. That is why they tried to kill Him — because He was claiming to be the Man of Isaiah 11 and Isaiah 61, the Messiah, both man and God.

His delight was in the fear of the Lord (v. 3) — a joyful, not a servile fear — demonstrated throughout His life and ministry.

## The Work: Righteous Judgment

Verses 3-5: "He shall not judge by the sight of His eyes, nor decide by the hearing of His ears; but with righteousness He shall judge the poor, and decide with equity for the meek of the earth." He will judge according to what is in the heart. John 2:24-25 says Jesus did not commit Himself to men because He knew what was in men.

How will you survive that judgment? Romans tells us that God in Jesus is not just the judge but also the justifier. He says: "Father, count their sins against Me. When the time comes for Your wrath to fall upon them, pour it upon Me. I will take the infinite weight of Your displeasure against sin, because these are My people."

He is doubly belted in verse 5 — righteousness and faithfulness as His twin belts — meaning He is ready to act on our behalf at any moment.

## The Kingdom: Renewed Creation

Verses 6-9 describe the wolf and lamb, the leopard and goat, the child playing at the serpent's hole — creation restored. Notice in verse 8 who is playing with the serpents: a child. The child is the seed of the woman. Genesis 3:15 promised that the seed of the woman would crush the serpent's head. The enmity has ended because the serpent has been defeated.

Old Testament prophets often did not distinguish between the first and second coming of Christ — for them it was one singular work of the Messiah. Paul in Romans 8 says creation waits for the redemption of the body, when the curse of sin is fully lifted. That redemption was guaranteed by the Christmas story — by the birth, life, death, and resurrection of the Lord Jesus Christ. Christ only had to come and die once for all of this to be certain.

## The Banner and the Rest

Verse 10: "There shall be a Root of Jesse who shall stand as a banner to the people; for the Gentiles shall seek Him, and His resting place shall be glorious."

The banner draws the nations. What is it that attracts all people to Christ? Jesus said: "If I be lifted up, I will attract all people to Myself." The banner is the cross. The throne of our King is an instrument of death. That is the banner we carry.

And the Branch provides rest. Jesus says in Matthew 11:28-30: "Come to Me, all you who labor and are heavy-laden, and I will give you rest. Take My yoke upon you and learn from Me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. For My yoke is easy and My burden is light."

The branch of Jesse has come. If you are an unbeliever in our midst, this is where you need to be. Come to the One who is the banner. Come to Him who has been lifted up on the cross for sinners such as yourself. Today is the day to enter into the rest prepared for those who embrace the Branch of Jesse.
